title: Preparing items for a repair cafe visit
subtitle: What repair cafes can and cannot fix, how to prepare your item, and what to expect on the day
excerpt: Repair cafes are volunteer-run events where skilled fixers attempt free repairs on household items. A prepared visit is more likely to result in a successful repair than arriving with a bag of unexamined broken things. This explains what repair cafes typically accept, how to diagnose and document the fault, and what to bring to give the fixer the best chance.
sourceNotes: Repair Cafe UK network guidance (public). Restart Project guidance on preparing electronics for repair. WRAP guidance on extending product lifetimes. Recycle Now item longevity resources.

content.text: The UK has over 350 repair cafes in the Repair Cafe International network, plus a larger number of community repair events, Restart Party events, and tool libraries with repair sessions. Most operate on a drop-in basis on fixed weekday or weekend sessions. Fixers are volunteers; repairs are free. Success rates depend heavily on the complexity of the fault and the quality of the item presented.
Small electrical appliances: toasters, kettles, lamps, radios, fans, hairdryers. These are the most common repair cafe intake and typically have the highest fix rate.
Clothing and textiles: mending, darning, zip and button replacement, hemming. A sewing volunteer can usually handle these quickly.
Bicycles: punctures, brake adjustment, gear adjustment, cable replacement.
Furniture and wooden items: broken joints, chipped veneers, loose chair legs.
Ceramics and jewellery: breaks and snapped joins.
What repair cafes generally cannot fix
Large white goods (washing machines, fridges): too large and too specialist. Some Restart Project events cover these with advance booking.
Proprietary electronics that are sealed and unrepairable by design (many modern laptops, phones with glued screens). Some Restart events have phone repair kits, but this is not universal.
Items with mould, infestations, or hazardous materials (asbestos gaskets in old appliances, for example). Fixers are volunteers and are not obliged to handle unsafe items.
Clean the item. A toaster full of crumbs, a greasy fan, or a muddy bag takes fixer time to deal with before they can diagnose the fault. Clean items get repaired faster.

Bring all the parts. For an electrical item with a detachable cable or removable components, bring the whole set. A fixer cannot test a lamp without the bulb, or a food processor without the bowl and blade.

Write down the fault. Note exactly what happens: the appliance trips the circuit, the appliance powers on but doesn't heat, the sound it makes. A written fault description saves conversation time and is more accurate than trying to recall it under pressure at the table.
Bring the original manual or the model number. Fixers diagnosing unfamiliar brands benefit from access to circuit diagrams or part numbers. Even a photo of the model label on your phone is useful.
If you have already sourced a replacement part (a fuse, a power switch, a zip), bring it. Repair cafes may not stock specific components; providing the part removes the main bottleneck.

Most repair cafes operate a queue system. Popular events fill quickly; arriving early is advisable. Fixers are volunteers, not technicians under contract. If an item cannot be repaired, this is a judgment call, not a guarantee of failure. A second repair cafe in a different area may have a fixer with different skills. The Restart Project and iFixit both maintain guides for self-repair of common items if the cafe visit does not succeed.

attrs.symptom: Fixer says the item is beyond repair
attrs.cause: Component is unavailable, damage is too extensive, or the item is not repairable by design
attrs.fix: Ask whether a self-repair is possible with the right part (iFixit guides cover many branded items). If genuinely unrepairable, check whether the manufacturer runs a take-back scheme before sending to general recycling.
